summary: Jeff Atwood explores his decade-long journey with rechargeable batteries, from discovering NiMH technology through a 1998 John Dvorak column to understanding the pitfalls of modern marketing claims. He reveals that while battery capacity has nearly doubled in ten years, manufacturers hide the 'dirty little secret' of self-discharge rates that can drain batteries within a month. The post recommends hybrid or pre-charged varieties like Sanyo Eneloop over high-capacity marketing gimmicks, and advocates for the La Crosse BC-900 charger as the solution to poor charging practices that destroy good batteries. Atwood concludes that informed consumers armed with proper technology can overcome the gadget world's battery demands.
key_points: Battery capacity doubled from 1300mAh to 2500mAh between 1998-2008, but this metric alone is misleading
Self-discharge rates of 20-30% per month for NiMH batteries are the hidden factor manufacturers don't advertise
'Hybrid' or 'pre-charged' battery varieties offer much better real-world performance than high-capacity marketing claims suggest
Most bundled battery chargers are 'junk' that destroy good batteries through improper charging
Smart chargers with individual battery control and multiple modes can revitalize even marginal batteries
money_quotes: Do the math: It's like buying 1,000 alkaline batteries for less than 10 bucks. Imagine what this does to the lucrative disposable-battery business.
There is a dirty little secret that they don't want you to hear: self-discharge rate.
So putting a larger number on the box is ultimately a method of fooling consumers with marketing. Where have we seen that before? Oh right, everywhere.
The fast chargers, especially the cheap ones, excel at one thing: destroying perfectly good batteries.
I spent several hours after I got it plugging various batteries in it, trying different modes, and watching it work.
I'm not sure what the exact definition of geek is, but I think 'enjoys recharging batteries' has to be very high on that list.
key_insight: Marketing metrics like battery capacity are meaningless without understanding the hidden technical realities like self-discharge rates, and informed consumers need quality tools to overcome industry deception.
